items.q: What dried fruit is made from a plum?
items.explanation: Prunes are dried plums, while raisins are dried grapes.

items.q: A cube has six faces.
items.explanation: A cube is a three-dimensional solid with six equal square faces, twelve edges, and eight vertices.

items.q: Which day of the week comes after Tuesday?
items.explanation: In the standard seven-day week sequence, Wednesday follows Tuesday.

items.q: What is the name of the imaginary line at 0 degrees latitude?
items.explanation: The Equator divides Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres and is the reference line for latitude.

items.q: What is water called when it is in solid form?
items.explanation: When water molecules freeze into a crystalline structure, the solid phase is called ice.

items.q: What appliance is designed to brown slices of bread?
items.explanation: A toaster applies radiant heat to sliced bread and can brown each side in separate slots.

items.q: Owls are mammals.
items.explanation: Owls are birds of prey, so they have feathers, beaks, and lay eggs rather than nursing their young.

items.q: What tool is commonly used to cut paper?
items.explanation: Scissors use two pivoted blades to shear materials such as paper and thin cardboard.

items.q: What is a baby goat called?
items.explanation: Kid is the standard term for a young goat in animal husbandry and zoology.

items.q: What do you call a person whose profession is writing books?
items.explanation: An author creates the original text of a work, while editors and publishers handle revision and production.

items.q: Which empire was ruled by Mansa Musa in the 14th century?
items.explanation: Mansa Musa, famous for his wealth and pilgrimage to Mecca, ruled the Mali Empire in West Africa.

items.q: A palindrome reads the same backward and forward.
items.explanation: Words such as level and racecar are common examples of palindromes because their letters reverse symmetrically.

items.q: Which playwright wrote A Doll's House?
items.explanation: A Doll's House premiered in 1879 and is one of Ibsen's best-known realist dramas.

items.q: What term describes precipitation made acidic by atmospheric sulfur and nitrogen compounds?
items.explanation: Acid rain forms when sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides react with water vapor in the atmosphere.

items.q: Which type of blood cell carries most oxygen in human blood?
items.explanation: Red blood cells contain hemoglobin, the protein that binds and transports oxygen.

items.q: What data structure processes items in first-in, first-out order?
items.explanation: A queue removes elements in the same order they were added, which models waiting-line behavior.

items.q: The ISBN system is used to identify books.
items.explanation: An ISBN is an International Standard Book Number used by publishers and retailers to identify a specific edition of a book.

items.q: What strait at the southern tip of South America is named after Ferdinand Magellan?
items.explanation: The Strait of Magellan provided an early navigational route between the Atlantic and Pacific before the Panama Canal.

items.q: What famous ancient library was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
items.explanation: The Library of Alexandria became a symbol of classical scholarship and large-scale manuscript collection.

items.q: What 1967 agreement bans placing nuclear weapons in Earth's orbit?
items.explanation: The Outer Space Treaty set foundational rules for peaceful use of outer space and prohibited WMDs in orbit.

items.q: Which legal principle bars a second trial for the same offense after an acquittal?
items.explanation: Double jeopardy protections are embedded in criminal law systems to prevent repeated prosecution for one offense.

items.q: The Peace of Augsburg established the principle cuius regio, eius religio in the Holy Roman Empire.
items.explanation: The 1555 Peace of Augsburg allowed rulers within the Holy Roman Empire to determine whether their territories would follow Catholicism or Lutheranism.

items.q: Which philosopher wrote Being and Time?
items.explanation: Being and Time was published in 1927 and became a major work in 20th-century continental philosophy.

items.q: What is the name of the point in an Earth orbit that is farthest from Earth?
items.explanation: For Earth-centered orbits, apogee is the farthest point and perigee is the nearest point.

items.q: In molecular biology, what are non-coding sequences removed from pre-mRNA called?
items.explanation: Introns are spliced out during RNA processing, while exons are joined to form mature mRNA.

items.q: Which 1648 settlement ended the Thirty Years' War in Europe?
items.explanation: The settlement included treaties signed at Munster and Osnabruck and reshaped European state sovereignty.

items.q: Bayes' theorem concerns conditional probability.
items.explanation: Bayes' theorem updates the probability of a hypothesis based on new evidence by relating conditional probabilities.

items.q: In music theory, what interval spans three whole tones?
items.explanation: A tritone equals six semitones and historically earned the nickname diabolus in musica in some contexts.

items.q: What is the SI base unit of luminous intensity?
items.explanation: Candela is an SI base unit, while lumen and lux are derived photometric units.

items.q: What term describes words made from initials that are pronounced letter by letter, such as FBI?
items.explanation: Initialisms are spoken as individual letters, while acronyms are usually pronounced as words.
